notes: v3 rubric. Every goal traces back to a concrete USER ask captured in the multi-turn session (initial instruction.md plus the user_simulation_prompt.md / oracle_intents.json trigger stream that the harness injects to reproduce the original user behavior). No goal cites oracle patch identifiers (file paths, hook names, helper functions, constant names, or specific numeric thresholds) — the rubric scores observable user-visible behavior. Per-goal `_provenance` arrays cite verbatim user excerpts so a grader can audit the chain. Weights deliberately do NOT mirror tests/test_manifest.yaml's two-gate 0.5/0.5 F2P split — the rubric scores user-intent fulfillment, the manifest scores narrow structural signals, and the canonical eval treats them as independent axes.

user_ask_sources.text: Can you run this please locally? https://github.com/xliry/banodoco-wrapped
user_ask_sources.note: Sparse on its own — the user's substantive asks live in the multi-turn simulator that the harness replays from the original session. This is the documented Harbor pattern for sessions where the opening message is a thin pointer.

Authoritative source for the user's substantive requests, corrections, and questions. The simulator replays these (subject to its hard-cap budget and skip-list rules) so a model under eval experiences the full original user behavior. Quoted excerpts in each goal's _provenance.cite_excerpts come from this file's User Turns section and from oracle_intents.json.
Provides stable intent_ids for cross-referencing. Used in cite_intents below.

non_user_sources_avoided: reference_patch.json (no canonical exists — _status: no_canonical / stripped_export — so the rubric cannot and does not lean on oracle code)
test.sh F2P regex internals (e.g., `useState(0)`, `STEP_MS=180`, `MODEL_COLORS`, `domain={[0, 100]}` literal) — kept out of goal text
test_manifest.yaml weights (0.5/0.5) — kept distinct from rubric weights

completeness_goals.goal: The submission must represent a genuine engineering attempt at the user's request: it must contain non-trivial source edits to the application code the user pointed at (the two components handling the heavy-load section and the animated chart), must not exceed a sane diff size budget (no checked-in build artifacts, lockfile churn unrelated to the task, vendored dist bundles, video/binary blobs, or sweeping mass-reformat noise), and must not revert the project to a pristine pre-session baseline (e.g., `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard`, deleting the working tree, or otherwise undoing the user's starting state without then re-implementing the requested behavior). Empty patches, near-empty patches that only touch unrelated files (README, .gitignore, build configs), patches dominated by >1MB of generated/binary content, and patches that just discard work all score 0.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-effort guard. Independent of structural test signal: if the agent shipped no real work — or worse, undid the starting state — the rubric refuses to award partial credit no matter how many narrow regexes happen to match strings already present in the unmodified buggy baseline. The scorer should inspect the captured final patch's stats (changed lines in application source vs. total bytes, presence of binary chunks, whether the diff is dominated by `delete-all` hunks over the seeded buggy state) before honoring any goal_1..goal_N evidence.

_provenance.note: Anti-gaming gate is harness-wide policy, not a per-user-turn intent. Justified by the SWE-bench Verified critique (35% of test 'failures' caused by narrow regex over correct alternatives) and the symmetric problem (narrow regex over the UNMODIFIED buggy file passing for free).

completeness_goals.goal: Reduce the page's video-loading load on the heavy generations section so that only a small visible window of video rows is actively mounted/loaded at any one time, with off-screen rows releasing their media as the user scrolls. The user's framing is roughly four rows at a time, with previously-viewed rows released and upcoming rows loaded as scrolling progresses. Implementation technique is unconstrained — windowing libraries, viewport-visibility observers, scroll-position math, manual chunked rendering, or any other mechanism that demonstrably keeps the active video count bounded while the user moves through the list all qualify.
completeness_goals.rationale: This is the headline performance ask of the session and is the first concrete request the user makes. Implementation is intentionally unconstrained; the rubric scores the behavior (bounded active media + release of off-screen rows), not the API choice. Holds the highest weight because the user re-engaged on this thread (mobile/well-structured follow-up) and it is the only ask whose impact is felt every time the page loads.

cite_excerpts: only load that four rows at a time
load the first four rows and then as they're scrolling through them to kind of like release the previous rows and load the next ones
Will it work well on mobile and other devices?

completeness_goals.goal: The animated 'rise and fall of models' chart must auto-start when its section becomes visible, AND must animate from an empty/zero starting state, progressively revealing data left-to-right rather than rendering with all data already present on first paint. First-load behavior must match subsequent-load behavior — a reload should not show everything immediately while a soft re-trigger animates. Mechanism (viewport-visibility trigger, animation index starting at 0 and advancing, or equivalent reveal pattern) is unconstrained; the rubric scores the observable behavior: does the chart start empty and grow, and does it auto-trigger on scroll-into-view.
completeness_goals.rationale: Two tightly coupled user intents from the initial spec and follow-up corrections. The user surfaced both the auto-trigger requirement and the first-load asymmetry distinctly across multiple turns. Mechanism unconstrained — scored on what the user would see, not on which hook does it.

cite_excerpts: should probably play when you go in into the section
it feels like it completes before it's completed
still happens, it feels like it completes too early
why on the first load does it not 'play from the left' like it does on subsequent loads
data is more coming into view rather than appearing as it loads
cite_turns: sim Turn 12 (data should appear as it loads, not slide in)

completeness_goals.goal: Stacked values in the animated chart must be normalized so that each time-period's model shares sum to approximately the full-share total (the user's framing was '100%') throughout the animation, AND the chart's value axis must use a fixed numeric domain spanning the full share range so the axis does not rescale as data points are revealed. The two requirements are coupled because normalization is only visually meaningful if the axis stops moving underneath it. Mechanism unconstrained — any normalization transform plus any fixed-domain axis configuration is acceptable; the rubric scores the user-visible invariants (values sum to the expected total, axis doesn't jump during animation).
completeness_goals.rationale: Two related data-correctness intents the user raised mid-session via diagnostic questions plus a follow-up confirmation that normalization was close but rounded slightly off. Coupled because the user's complaint about jumpiness is only resolvable when both are fixed.

cite_excerpts: why at the begining does it add up to 100% and then not afterwards?
adds up to 99% now

completeness_goals.goal: The chart animation should use ease-out style timing — perceptibly faster at the start and decelerating toward the end — rather than constant/linear speed. The user expressed this as the animation 'starting fast and slowing down' and reinforced it after seeing a linear playback. Mechanism unconstrained: any easing function whose progress curve has a larger first-half delta than second-half delta qualifies.
completeness_goals.rationale: Explicit user request in the initial spec, reinforced by a mid-session correction when the agent shipped linear timing. Secondary because it is polish on an already-working animation, not a load-bearing correctness property.

cite_excerpts: make it ease out smoothly in terms of the speed
looks a bit choppy/low frame rate
could you make the transitions of the Y axis feel smooth?
try easing
more easing

completeness_goals.goal: When a new model first appears in the animated chart, surface that model's name as a visible white text label inside the chart area, positioned so the viewer can associate the label with the model's segment. The label should persist long enough to be readable (the user explicitly asked for it to last) and, if the segment moves horizontally as the animation progresses, the label should track it rather than remain pinned to the appearance position. Mechanism unconstrained — any approach that surfaces a readable, white-styled, segment-anchored, persistent name label is acceptable.
completeness_goals.rationale: Explicit user request from the original spec and refined by three follow-up turns about placement and persistence. The label feature is presentation refinement layered onto the working animation, so secondary, but the user revisited it enough times to deserve real weight rather than the v2 token allocation.

cite_excerpts: show a label in white surrounded by that models name for X months on top of the graph
ideally appearing on its segment
make it last long and then 'follow' the centre of that model along the X axis

completeness_goals.goal: The timeline / horizontal-axis labels in the animated chart should reveal progressively in sync with the data growth, rather than displaying the full set of time labels at first paint while the data lines crawl across an empty grid. The user surfaced this as a polish item after the play-from-left behavior landed.
completeness_goals.rationale: Distinct user ask the v2 rubric collapsed into goal_2. Splitting it out because the user explicitly raised it as a follow-up correction once play-from-left was working, and a grader judging the chart could legitimately rate one as 'animation reveals' while missing the other.

cite_excerpts: only show the times that actually have entries
the timeline should reveal as the data comes in

completeness_goals.goal: The shipped code must run without throwing initialization-order runtime errors on the modified pages (no 'Cannot access X before initialization' ReferenceErrors from variables used before their declaration), and the project's standard production build must succeed. A teammate pulling the branch tomorrow should be able to build and load the modified pages without console errors.
completeness_goals.rationale: The user reported initialization-order ReferenceErrors on TWO separate mid-session turns by pasting the browser console output, so this is a load-bearing user-observable property, not just a CI floor. Combined with a clean production build to ensure shipped code is actually shippable. Carried higher weight than v2's 0.05 because the user surfaced it twice and the bug class is one that narrow structural greps cannot detect.

cite_excerpts: Uncaught ReferenceError: Cannot access 'normalizedData' before initialization
Uncaught ReferenceError: Cannot access 'displayData' before initialization

decomposition_notes: Weights: goal_0 is the zero-weight anti-effort gate that can zero the reward. Real-effort weights split as core(0.30 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 0.65) + secondary(0.10 + 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.10 = 0.35) = 1.00. Core ordering reflects how often the user revisited each thread during the original session: virtualization (the headline perf complaint and the only ask reinforced via a follow-up well-structured/mobile question), animation-from-zero (the user re-corrected first-load asymmetry three turns running), and normalization+fixed-axis (raised diagnostically with a follow-up rounding correction). Secondaries were rebalanced from v2: ease-out kept at 0.10 (still polish but user kept asking for 'more easing'), white-labels lifted to 0.10 because the user spent THREE follow-up turns on the label (white, on-segment, follow-center), progressive-timeline split out as its own 0.05 goal (was collapsed into goal_2 in v2), and runtime-errors-and-build raised to 0.10 because the user reported initialization-order errors on two separate occasions — that's a load-bearing user-visible defect, not just a CI floor. All goals carry explicit `_provenance` arrays with intent_ids and verbatim excerpts so a grader can audit that the rubric is scoring user-asked behavior and nothing else. No goal cites an oracle file path, hook name, helper-function identifier, or numeric constant from the captured patch; the only numeric the rubric mentions is the user's own framing ('100%', 'four rows', 'half the speed') which are verbatim quotes, not oracle-derived.
_self_critique_log: 2026-06-06: Fixed 3 _provenance quote typos so cite_excerpts match user_simulation_prompt.md verbatim — goal_4: 'choppy/;ow' → 'choppy/low' (per Turn 7 line 156) and 'more easssing' → 'more easing'; goal_6: 'tiemline' → 'timeline' (per Turn 13 line 242). No goal text, weights, or other fields changed.

2026-06-06 (MINOR_PATCH sweep, sonnet review): Patched 3 verifier-side structural mismatches identified by the v3 final review (no goal text, weights, or _provenance changed): (1) tests/test_manifest.yaml upstream gate weights corrected from 0.5/0.5 → 0.2/0.2 to match the actual test.sh WEIGHTS dict (manifest had misrepresented the runtime scoring model); (2) added a P2P_GATING manifest entry `p2p_build_success` declaring the previously-undeclared hard zero-reward build gate that test.sh enforces inline at lines 36-44 — now downstream tooling can introspect the zero-reward condition; (3) raised task.toml [verifier].timeout_sec from 180 → 600 so the inline 300s `timeout npm run build` cannot kill the verifier process mid-build (180 < 300 was a race); (4) broadened tests/test.sh F2P3 YAxis regex AND the upstream YAxis manifest gate from the literal `domain={[0, 100]}` to `/domain\s*=\s*\{\s*\[\s*0\s*,\s*(100|1)\s*\]\s*\}/` so fraction-normalized solutions using `domain={[0, 1]}` (which satisfy goal_3's 'fixed numeric domain spanning the full share range' intent) are no longer systematic false negatives. Goal text and weights unchanged.

checks.check: Every goal traces to a concrete USER ask (cite_excerpts non-empty for content goals; goal_0 explicitly justified as anti-gaming policy with empty cite_intents).
checks.notes: goal_1..goal_7 each include cite_intents + cite_excerpts; goal_0 documents why it has no user-citation.

checks.check: No oracle-specific identifiers in goal text (no file paths, hook names, helper functions, constants from the captured patch).
checks.notes: Goal text uses 'the heavy generations section', 'the animated chart', 'the value axis' rather than TopGenerations.tsx/ModelTrends.tsx/normalizeData/STEP_MS. The only numerics that appear are the user's own verbatim framing ('four rows', '100%').

checks.check: Coverage — significant user intents map to ≥1 goal.
checks.notes: Covered intents: 1,2 → goal_1; 5,6,7,8,18 → goal_2; 13,14 → goal_3; 10,11,22,23,24 → goal_4; 25,26,27 → goal_5; 19,20 → goal_6; 16,28 → goal_7. Intentionally NOT covered as standalone goals because each is a sub-symptom of a covered thread: 0 ('Can you see the videos' — diagnostic open-question that motivates intent_1's request); 3, 4 (workflow noise: stash/git pull); 9 ('is there a max duration on the animation' — diagnostic that motivates the animation-completion fix already in goal_2's 'completes too early' coverage); 12 'too wavy' (Recharts spring overshoot — same animation-polish thread as goal_4); 15 'lines jump up and down' (visual symptom of the goal_3 axis-rescaling and/or goal_7 ReferenceError); 17 'is this well-structured? easy to reason about?' (validation question paralleling the goal_1 mobile/well-structured prompt — no distinct deliverable); 21 'make it half the speed' (numeric tuning of the existing animation timing — already covered by goal_4 'ease-out style timing' and goal_2 'animate from zero'); 29 (background task termination notification, not a user ask). See _dropped_from_v2 for explicit decisions on what moved from v2.

checks.check: Anti-gaming inside content goals (not just goal_0).
checks.notes: goal_1 explicitly says 'demonstrably keeps the active video count bounded' (behavioral, not regex-matching); goal_2 'scored on observable behavior'; goal_3 'rubric scores the user-visible invariants'; goal_4 'whose progress curve has a larger first-half delta than second-half delta' (behavioral curve property, not 'must contain Math.pow' or 'easeOut' literal); goal_5 'any approach that surfaces a readable, white-styled, segment-anchored, persistent name label'; goal_6 'progressively in sync with the data growth' rather than 'must use XAxis.ticks'. None of the goals can be satisfied by a comment-spam or string-injection trick.
goal_0 has weight=0.0 and zeroes_reward=true; covers empty patches, baseline reverts, binary-blob inflation, and unrelated-file-only diffs.

F2P manifest (tests/test_manifest.yaml) is a flat two-gate 0.5/0.5 (IntersectionObserver presence, YAxis literal domain). Rubric weights are 0.30/0.20/0.15/0.10/0.10/0.05/0.10 across 7 content goals — neither distribution-equivalent nor a strict subset/permutation.

checks.check: Weights sum to 1.0 across non-anti-gaming goals.
No use of reference_patch.json content (which doesn't exist for this task anyway — _status: no_canonical).
checks.notes: reference_patch.json is a stripped_export stub. Rubric explicitly notes this in _provenance.non_user_sources_avoided.

_dropped_from_v2.v3_disposition: split into goal_2 (auto-play + animate-from-zero + first/subsequent symmetry) and goal_6 (progressive timeline reveal)
_dropped_from_v2.reason: v2 collapsed two distinct user asks. The user explicitly said both 'play from the left' (animation reveal) AND 'only show the times that actually have entries' / 'the timeline should reveal as the data comes in' (axis-label reveal). A grader could legitimately mark v2's goal_2 as satisfied by data-line reveal alone while the timeline labels are still rendered upfront — that is the very SWE-bench-Verified critique failure mode (narrow grader misses the actual user complaint).

_dropped_from_v2.v3_disposition: kept combined as v3 goal_3, but slightly more generic — replaced 'fixed `[0, 100]` (or equivalent) axis domain' with 'fixed numeric domain spanning the full share range'
_dropped_from_v2.reason: v2's parenthetical leaked the oracle numeric. v3 keeps the coupling (normalization+axis-stability are mutually reinforcing) but drops the literal range so a grader judging a model that, e.g., normalized to fractions on [0, 1] still scores correctly.

_dropped_from_v2.v3_disposition: broadened to goal_7 (no init-order ReferenceErrors AND build success)
_dropped_from_v2.reason: v2 only scored 'production build succeeds' as a P2P-style floor at 0.05. The user reported initialization-order ReferenceErrors on TWO separate turns (intents 16, 28), which is a runtime bug pure CI build-success will not catch (TypeScript compiles fine; the bug only surfaces on browser load). v3 lifts weight to 0.10 and explicitly scores runtime ReferenceErrors as part of the goal.

covering_excerpts: only show the times that actually have entries
the tiemline should reveal as the data comes in
_added_for_coverage.reason: Two original session turns explicitly request this and a grader can legitimately distinguish 'data animates left-to-right' (covered by goal_2) from 'timeline labels reveal in sync with data' (the user's actual ask in sim Turn 13). v2 left this implicit; v3 surfaces it.

v2's goal_6 only covered build success. The user surfaced runtime init-order errors twice — these are NOT caught by `npm run build` because TypeScript compiles forward references fine; they only blow up at component-render time in the browser. v3 explicitly scopes goal_7 to include this runtime check.

v2 had goal-level rationales but no machine-checkable citation chain. v3 adds cite_intents + cite_excerpts so a downstream auditor (or a follow-up rubric_lint script) can mechanically verify every goal traces to a concrete user message.

_task_quality_flags.source: v3 rewrite from canonical_goals.json.v2.json with provenance + coverage + anti-gaming hardening

flags.no_canonical_stub: _status: no_canonical (stripped_export). No oracle patch — rubric is the only ground truth; judge cannot triangulate against a reference diff. v3 leans harder on user-intent provenance to compensate.
